coredata.dc:title: The effect of functionalization on rice-husks derived carbon quantum dots properties and cadmium removal
coredata.dc:creator: Zainal Abidin, Nur Hafizah
Wongso, Viona
Hui, Khee Chung
Cho, Kuk
Sambudi, Nonni Soraya
Ang, Wei Lun
Saad, Bahruddin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, functionalized CQDs have been synthesized from rice husks through hydrothermal process by varying the amount of Ethylenediamine (EDA) and ascorbic acid as sources of amine group and carboxyl group, respectively. HRTEM images show the formation of functionalized CQDs with sizes less than 10 nm. XRD analysis shows that CQDs have graphitic structure of (002) plane while their thermal stabilities were analyzed using TGA. FTIR analysis of functionalized CQDs reveals the presence of oxygen containing groups which indicates their higher affinity towards cationic binding. UV Visible spectra show highest peak at around 240 nm which corresponds to electronic transition of CC bond. In addition, XPS analysis shows that atomic percentages of carbon and nitrogen increase when using higher amount of ascorbic acid and EDA. Photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs indicate that increasing amount of functionalization agent can tune the intensity, with emission in green region wavelength (495−570 nm). Meanwhile, quenching of PL intensity and reduction in AAS absorbance show that carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs have the potential in detection of cadmium and more than 70 % removal of cadmium from aqueous solution. The cadmium removal fits the pseudo-second order kinetic model and Freundlich isotherm with kinetic rate of 0.0000243 gmg−1 min−1 and maximum adsorption capacity of 332.72 mg/g, respectively.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, functionalized CQDs have been synthesized from rice husks through hydrothermal process by varying the amount of Ethylenediamine (EDA) and ascorbic acid as sources of amine group and carboxyl group, respectively. HRTEM images show the formation of functionalized CQDs with sizes less than 10 nm. XRD analysis shows that CQDs have graphitic structure of (002) plane while their thermal stabilities were analyzed using TGA. FTIR analysis of functionalized CQDs reveals the presence of oxygen containing groups which indicates their higher affinity towards cationic binding. UV Visible spectra show highest peak at around 240 nm which corresponds to electronic transition of CC bond. In addition, XPS analysis shows that atomic percentages of carbon and nitrogen increase when using higher amount of ascorbic acid and EDA. Photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs indicate that increasing amount of functionalization agent can tune the intensity, with emission in green region wavelength (495−570 nm). Meanwhile, quenching of PL intensity and reduction in AAS absorbance show that carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs have the potential in detection of cadmium and more than 70 % removal of cadmium from aqueous solution. The cadmium removal fits the pseudo-second order kinetic model and Freundlich isotherm with kinetic rate of 0.0000243 gmg−1 min−1 and maximum adsorption capacity of 332.72 mg/g, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel class of carbon nanoparticles, initiated in 2004 during the extraction of single walled carbon nanotubes [1]. These nanoparticles possess size range of less than 10 nm, have certain distinct properties such as high biocompatibility, less toxicity, high solubility in water, as well as unique fluorescence property [1]. CQDs have the properties that look alike graphene nanomaterials, possessing amorphous structure [2]. The characteristics of CQDs allow them to be an appropriate alternative in numerous implementations such as biosensing, bioimaging and photocatalysis. CQDs, also known as fluorescent nanoparticles have gained attention in certain studies due to their small sizes, adjustable fluorescence emission, high dispersibility and solubility in water, as well as chemically inert [1,2]. These carbon nanoparticles consist of -sp2 and -sp3 hybridized carbon atoms which allow for further investigation on their tunable properties [3]. There are various synthesis methods of CQDs including top down and bottom up approaches. Top down approaches such as electrochemical and laser ablation are usually not preferable due to their complexity and high cost [4].

Meanwhile, bottom up approaches that utilize organic wastes as precursor in an environmental friendly way are always recommended in preparing CQDs. Hydrothermal method, as one of the bottom up approaches has been implemented in synthesizing CQDs at different temperatures due to its convenience and controllable size of obtained CQDs [5]. Hydrothermal process is more favorable compared to other methods due to some advantages such as low cost, high efficiency and compatible with renewable organic resources [6]. In addition, hydrophilic and fluorescent carbon nanoparticles can be obtained from this synthesis route by selecting appropriate precursors as the source of carbon [7].

Recently, various resources such as glucose, citric acid and chitosan were utilized as precursors to prepare CQDs. In this particular study, rice husks are chosen as the carbon precursor in synthesizing CQDs as it is believed to be one of the green, facile and simple methods towards conserving the environment. Apart from that, the fact that rice husks consisted mainly of about 30%–50% carbon has become one of the reasons for them to be chosen as the precursor [3,4]. To add, rice husks have been one of the most organic wastes produced in the milling or dehusking process. Hence, an alternative to treat the waste was by converting the unused rice husks into valuable product [8]. During CQDs’ synthesis process, variation of hydrothermal temperature does give effect on the particle sizes of CQDs [9]. This condition is due to the fact that hydrothermal process consists of carbonization and dehydration of carbon core.

Apart from that, functionalization on CQDs’ surface gives high impact as it assists to enhance the performance of the carbon nanoparticles [10]. Functionalization provides more surface functional groups on CQDs’ structure and this characteristic allows CQDs to be utilized in broad applications [11]. Furthermore, functionalization agent assists in tuning the functional groups in CQDs’ structure as it enlarges the band gap between Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) for electron excitation [12]. Abdelsalam et al. [13] reported that functionalization of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) could increase the interaction with metal ions and tune the band gap. The enhanced fluorescence intensity of CQDs using functionalization agent also stated in a study by Li et al. [14], indicating that the difference in the band gap produced distinct properties of CQDs.

In addition, fluorescent CQDs have gained attention in applications of metal ions sensing and removal due to their wider absorption spectra and adjustable emission wavelength [15]. However, their applications in cadmium detection and removal were rarely investigated especially using rice husks derived CQDs. Cadmium, (Cd2+) has been classified as one of the heavy metals that causes pollution to the environment as well as affecting human health [16]. In a previous study by Shtepliuk et al. [17], cadmium physisorption, known as adsorption, happened on the surface of carbon nanoparticles. Furthermore, cadmium removal by using natural adsorbent could achieve beyond 96 % after contact time of 90 min as reported in a study by Kuchekar et al. [18]. Therefore, the utilization of carbon nanoparticles through adsorption can be an alternative in metal ion removal due to the facile and simple technique in CQDs' synthesis from organic precursor.

The amount of functionalization agent also affects the distinct properties of CQDs in terms of their fluorescence and binding energy. The photoluminescence properties with respect to different amount of functionalization agent during hydrothermal reaction are yet to be explored. Therefore, in this work, variation in amount of functionalization agents which are EDA and ascorbic acid during hydrothermal process are expected to produce CQDs with tunable properties. Subsequently, the functionalized CQDs with optimum amount of EDA and ascorbic acid will be further utilized in removal of cadmium from its aqueous solution.

Rice husks were obtained from Jabatan Pertanian Negeri Perak, Malaysia while Hydrochloric acid (HCl, ̴ 37 %, Code: H/1200/PC17) was purchased from Fisher Scientific U.K. Ethylenediamine (CAS-No 107-15-3) was purchased from Merck Germany while Ascorbic acid (CAS-No 50-81-7) was purchased from HmbG Chemicals. Cadmium Nitrate Tetrahydrate (CAS-No 10022-68-1) and Sodium Hydroxide (CAS-NO 1310-73-2) were purchased from Acros Organics and Fischer Scientific U.K., respectively. Sodium Chloride (CAS-No-7647-14-5) was also purchased from Fischer Scientific U.K. All chemicals were utilized without further purification. De-ionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) from PureLab Flex was utilized for the whole experiment.

Rice husks were pretreated before CQDs’ synthesis to ease carbonization during hydrothermal process [8]. 1 g of rice husk was washed with DI water to eliminate any residues. Then, wet rice husks were dried in oven at 60 °C before blended into powders. To remove inorganic impurities, the blended rice husks were cleansed with HCl (0.1 M) before being centrifuged at 4000 rpm [19]. The treated rice husks were again rinsed in DI water and dried in the oven at 60 °C.

Temperature of 190 °C was used for the hydrothermal process based on previous study by Lin et al. [20] to synthesize both amino and carboxyl functionalized CQDs, each denoted as NCQD and CCQD. EDA and ascorbic acid with volume variation (2.5 ml–10 ml) were used as functionalization agents for amino and carboxyl groups, respectively. 100 mg of treated rice husk powders and varied amount of ascorbic acid and EDA were transferred into Teflon lined autoclave to undergo hydrothermal process for about 12 h. After reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature for 24 h before filtered by vacuum filter. For further purification, CQDs were dialyzed against DI water in Visking tubing (Cellulose 10 M) for 24 h. The obtained functionalized CQDs were characterized with HRTEM, FTIR, UV–vis, PL, and XPS analysis. The CQDs samples were denoted by CCQD-2.5 to CCQD-10.0 for carboxyl functionalized with respect to different amount of ascorbic acid in ml while NCQD-2.5 to NCQD-10.0 for amino functionalized with respect to varied amount of EDA in ml.

Perkin Elmer Spectrum One Spectrophotometer was used for Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis while UV–vis spectra of the samples were obtained using Agilent Technologies Cary Series UV–vis. In addition, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using Maya2000 Pro Spectrometer with 390 nm wavelength of excitation. The high resolution imaging of each sample was performed using Zeiss Libra 200FE High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) and the average particle sizes were determined using ImageJ software (measurement of 60 particles was analyzed). X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was done using PANalytical X'Pert Pro diffractometer with 0.02° step size while thermal stability of CQDs was analyzed using TA Instruments Q-5015 thermogravimetric analyzer using nitrogen from 25 to 800 °C with a scan rate of 10 °C per minute. On the other hand, X-Ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) analysis was performed using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha to determine the elemental composition in the samples.

Solutions of heavy metal ion (cadmium nitrate) with six different concentrations (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 ppm) were prepared to test the ability of functionalized CQDs in heavy metal ions detection. 2 ml of cadmium nitrate solution of each different concentration was mixed with 2 ml of functionalized CQDs in a beaker and then stirred at 200 rpm. The mixture was then analyzed using Photoluminescence (PL) Maya2000 Pro Spectrometer to observe the quenching of PL intensity with excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

Removal of heavy metal ions was evaluated by measuring the difference in concentration before and after adding functionalized CQDs into the cadmium nitrate solution (100 ppm) at different contact time. Firstly, 5 ml of cadmium nitrate (100 ppm) solution was mixed together with 2 ml of CQDs solution in a beaker for carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs, respectively. The solution was stirred at contact time of 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min. The effect of functionalized CQDs dosage was then determined by using different volume of both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs solution (1, 2 and 3 ml) mixed together with 2 ml of cadmium nitrate solution (100 ppm) to evaluate their removal efficiencies. The effect of initial concentration of cadmium solution was done by using variation of 25, 50, 75, 100, and 125 ppm of cadmium. On the other hand, the pH of cadmium solution was varied at 2, 6, 8, and 10 by adding 0.1 M of HCl or NaOH to observe the effect of solution’s pH towards cadmium removal. Salt addition method was done by adding different concentrations of NaCl (0.01, 0.1 and 1.0 M) to the cadmium solution. All the samples were agitated at 200 rpm. The performance of metal ion removal was determined using Agilent 240FS Atomic Absorption Spectrometer (AAS). The removal efficiency of cadmium and adsorption capacity can be expressed in the following Eqs. (1) and (2):

Where C0 and Ce are initial and remaining concentrations of cadmium solution (mg/L), respectively, m is the mass of adsorbent (g) and V is the volume of solution (L).

On the other hand, Point Zero Charge (PZC) of the adsorbent is determined by using salt addition method [21]. 3 ml of functionalized CQDs was added into 100 ml of NaCl (0.1 M), then the pH of the solution was adjusted to be 2,4,6,8, and 10 by adding 0.1 M of HCl or NaOH. The pH values were denoted as initial pH. Then, the set of solution was stirred at 200 rpm. The stabilized pH after 24 h was denoted as final pH. Finally, the graph of final pH versus initial pH was plotted to determine the PZC value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of functionalization agents

ce:section.ce:para: The HRTEM images of carboxyl (CCQD-2.5) and amino functionalized CQDs (NCQD-10.0) in Fig. 1 (a) and (c) indicate the successful formation of carbon dots. Functionalization process produces clearer spherical form of CQDs due to the binding of functional groups of –COOH from ascorbic acid and –NH2 from EDA to the structure of CQDs [22]. The morphologies of CQDs are obviously spherical with particle size distributions as shown in Fig. 1(b) and (d) for carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs, respectively. Carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs, each with average particle diameter of 3.89 ± 1.74 and 1.94 ± 1.67 nm, respectively, which indicate that CQDs’ structure tends to have smaller size than the non-functionalized CQDs due to the presence of functionalization agent. This can be attributed to the acidic and alkaline condition during synthesis in which they lead to protonation and deprotonation of CQDs, respectively. Both CCQDs and NCQDs have lattice spacing of 0.34 and 0.32 nm, respectively which correspond to typical (002) interlayer spacing of crystalline graphite [23]. The functionalization assists in tuning the size of CQDs and then will affect the electron density and quantum confinement effect in CQDs [24,25].

However, ascorbic acid may affect the pH of the CQDs’ structure in which protonation of H+ ions to the nanoparticles leads to the formation of larger particles. The variation of the normalized surface charge density with pH can be explained by the concentration of H + ions on the particle surface. As pH level increases, the bulk concentration of H + ions decreases, resulting in lower concentration of H + ions and consequently leads to a higher surface charge on the particle surface. Hence, in higher pH condition, if two strongly charged particles (NCQDs) get close together, they will repel each other and move in opposite directions. However, if two weakly charged (CCQDs) particles approach each other and get very close, then the van-der-Waals can become more attractive, making them stick together and this leads to aggregation or agglomerates, thereby a larger particle size is formed [25]. This explains the condition of CCQDs is larger than the NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD and TGA analyses of functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Diffraction patterns of carboxyl and amino functionalized CQD were obtained from XRD analysis as shown in Fig. 2 (a). The peaks centered at 2θ = ̴ 22.7° are attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon- based materials which reflect the nature of graphitic carbon [26,27]. This observation also relates to the highly disordered structure of carbon [27]. On the other hand, the Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was done to observe the thermal stability of the CQDs. The stability curves of the CQDs can be seen in Fig. 2(b). The initial weight losses (1.29 %) at temperature up to 150 °C are due to the evaporation of water molecules and other species that are weakly bounded on the CQDs’ surfaces [28,29]. This slight weight loss also suggests that the CQDs are stable up to this temperature. The weight losses in the temperature range of 200–500 °C also correspond to the losses of oxygen-containing groups [29]. Besides, the weight losses of 56.50 % at around 300–400 °C are attributed to those species that are anchored strongly with the CQDs and this confirms the surface functionalization on CQDs [30]. This observation also relates to the degradation of unreacted amine and acid groups in the CQDs [31]. Beyond the temperature of 750 °C, the path towards complete decomposition of CQDs can be observed in TGA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional groups on functionalized CQDs’ surface
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR analysis in Fig. 3 (a) was implemented to determine different additional groups when the CQDs were functionalized with both amino and carboxyl solutions. The broad band at around 3300 cm−1 corresponds to OH group in all samples synthesized by hydrothermal reaction. The observable peak at 1640 cm−1 indicates the presence of stretching vibrations of CO functional group [32]. There are amino containing groups present in all NCQD samples as can be observed at 1580 and 2800 cm-1 which correspond to NH bending and NH stretching, respectively. The NH stretching can be observed in the spectrum at around 3200−3500 cm−1 for NCQDs samples. This peak overlaps with the OH groups that may ascribed to the spectral contaminant due to absorbed water and hydrogen bonding. During hydrothermal process, rice husks have been destroyed to form carbon material containing aromatic structures rich in surface functional groups including hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. This also refers to carbonization process. When the amount of functionalization agent increases, OH or hydroxyl group also increases, hence leads to larger absorption band in the range of 3000−3500 cm−1.

UV–vis spectra in Fig. 3(b) show observable peak at around 240 nm due to π→π* electronic transition of CC bond [33]. In addition, visible region in the range of 400 nm–700 nm also shows absorption peak represented by each CQDs sample synthesized with different amount of functionalization agent [34]. The spectra indicate there is an absorption tail that extends to the visible region which attributed to the amine and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs [35]. In this region, the broadening peaks are observed which correspond to the size growth of fluorescent CQDs due to increased amount of functionalization agent. This also refers to the electronic structure to be modified by the effective orbital resonance of the amine moieties and the carbon core.

In visible light region, the peaks at around 450–550 nm tend to have blue shift when there is increment in the amount of functionalization agent. This observation is due to excitation of photo- excited electrons through the π–π* transitions that are proposed to relax into either the -sp2 energy levels or the surface states, giving rise to shorter or longer wavelength, respectively. The blue shift emission might bear discrete features owing to the quantum confinement effect (QCE) of electrons inside the -sp2 carbon domains [36]. The peak in visible region shows that the optical properties of CQDs also depend on the particle size in which in most cases, CQDs with size ranges of 1.5–3 nm exhibit visible region emission [23]. The distinct changes in emission wavelength show that optical properties of the nanoparticles come from the differences in the number of small -sp2 fragments of various sizes embedded in CQDs [37,38]. The properties can be attributed to the quantum confinement effect which is determined by the carbon core and also the surface state, which is determined by hybridization of the carbon backbone and connected chemical groups. Quantum confinement effect is highly related to the size of nanoparticles in which the luminescence energies are blue- shifted due to smaller particle sizes.

The surface state of the CQDs is commonly attributed to the surface modifications including functionalization of the CQDs [23,39]. When CQDs is illuminated with certain wavelength, a surface state emissive trap will dominate the emission. Most surface-state emission studies focused on the blue– green range emission [23]. A study by Zheng et al. [40] also proves that the emission of CQDs can be changed to blue region by surface reduction which comes from functionalization of the CQDs. The wavelength shifts also resulted from charge transfers between the functional groups and CQDs, which can tune the band gap of the CQDs. Based on the UV–vis spectra, it can be seen that a higher degree of modification, for example the increment of functionalization amount results in more surface defects, resulting in a blue-shift emission. In addition, carboxyl functionalized CQDs exhibit higher energy compared to amino functionalized CQDs due to higher electronegativity of carboxylic group [41]. The blue- shift emission also happens due to the formation of non- radiative group in acidic condition [42].

X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was implemented to determine the elemental analysis and related chemical bonding states in CQDs. XPS spectra in Fig. 4 (a–b) show the presence of three distinct peaks at binding energy of 285.0, 400.0 and 532.0 eV which correspond to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [37]. These peaks indicate the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements in the CQDs. The peak intensity of carbon element arises when the amount of ascorbic acid increases in carboxyl functionalized CQDs while there are clearer peaks at around 400 eV that correspond to existence of nitrogen element in amino functionalized CQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of C1s in Fig. 4(c–e) that correspond to the carbon features in CQDs are found to be at 285.0 and 288.0 eV which indicate the presence of CC and CO bonds, respectively [43].

Meanwhile, the N1s spectra of amino functionalized CQDs in Fig. 4(f–h) which show pronounced peaks at 399.8 and 401.7 eV, indicate the presence of nitrogen associated groups which are NC and NH bonds, respectively [44]. The presence of these elemental species agree with the FTIR results which indicate the CQDs are successfully functionalized with carboxyl and amine groups. There is no obvious changes in binding energy with variation of the amount of functionalization agent might be due to consistent hydrothermal temperature as shown in a study by Li et al. [14] for the synthesis reaction at temperature below 200 °C. The elemental analysis in terms of atomic percentage is presented in Table 1 . It can be seen that the carbon content in CQDs is higher when the amount of carboxyl groups increases while nitrogen content shows significant increment in amino functionalized CQDs. Oxygen element also shows higher percentage for both CQDs and this proves that CQDs contain high amount of oxygen- containing groups. The nitrogen content of less than 2 % in the CCQDs may ascribed to the components of rice husks. This is because rice husks contain around 0.05–4.26 % of nitrogen of their overall elemental composition [45]. Hence, the utilization of rice husks during hydrothermal leads to the occurrence of nitrogen element in the CCQDs even in a very small amount.

The photoluminescence properties also depend on the amount of functionalization agents for both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 5 (a), the PL intensity decreases upon increasing amount of ascorbic acid. However, when the amount of EDA increases, the PL intensity arises as shown in Fig. 5(b). This trend relates to the CQDs’ synthesis process in both acidic and alkaline conditions. In a previous study by Dutta Choudhury et al. [46], increasing amount of alkaline medium enhances photoluminescence intensity because more negatively charged CQDs are produced due to deprotonation effect. On the other hand, PL intensity tends to decrease in acidic medium due to protonation effect, thus producing larger particles in the solution [46].

Based on Fig. 5(a), in carboxyl functionalized CQDs, the PL intensity decreases upon adding the amount of ascorbic acid due to quenching effect. Photoluminescence intensity is reduced due to protonation to CQDs in acidic condition. Moreover, the condition of having lower PL intensity in increasing amount of ascorbic acid indicates the lower recombination of electrons and holes due to the surface defects in the CQDs [48]. Besides, the emission wavelength also increases upon acid addition. This is because high amount of acid enhances the protonation to the negatively charged CQDs and leads to stronger electrostatic interaction in CQDs’ structure [47]. Hence, agglomeration is enhanced causing the emission to favor red-shifted region with higher emission wavelength. The energy band gap also decreases for the electron to be excited from valence band to the conduction band [48]. Therefore, the result suggests that CCQD-2.5 is the optimum sample for further application as it exhibits highest PL intensity.

Meanwhile, PL spectra of amino functionalized CQDs in Fig. 5(b) show that PL intensity increases with the increasing amount of EDA. This causes the CQDs to be more negatively charged and thus, it enhances the PL intensity [14]. In addition, the emission peaks shift to lower emission wavelength when amount of EDA increases, indicating blue-shift wavelength region is favored. EDA also plays the role as nitrogen containing agent and according to previous study by Li et al. [14], this reaction will further enhance the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. Therefore, the result suggests that NCQD-10.0 is the optimum sample for further application as it exhibits highest PL intensity. Hence, both CCQD-2.5 and NCQD-10.0 were selected in further applications in cadmium detection and removal due to their higher PL intensities.

Carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs samples namely CCQD-2.5 and NCQD-10.0 were utilized for cadmium detection testing. Cadmium solutions were prepared at different concentrations and tested whether they extract noticeable changes in the PL intensity of both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs. The PL intensity tends to decrease when higher concentration of cadmium solution is mixed with CQDs solution. This condition implies the quenching phenomenon that occurred in the CQDs’ structure upon addition of heavy metal ions. The distinct optical response of both functionalized CQDs shows linear relation with the concentration of Cd2+ ions as shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). These observable changes are due to the electrostatic force of attraction or ionic force between Cd2+ cations and anionic CQDs that are abundant with oxygen containing groups, allowing them to bind together to form agglomerates [18].

In addition, Cd2+ ions gain electron from the ligand, hence reducing it to Cd. It can be seen that a PL quenching phenomenon occurs upon addition of cadmium solution. This is because Cd2+ ions are good electron acceptor, thus it is expected that the major factor that contributes to quenching is the electron transfer interaction between CQDs and the Cd2+ quencher. Furthermore, the presence of the quencher disturbs the recombination of electron and holes in the CQDs, hence leads to the decline of the intensity [49]. The emission wavelength also tends to be broader due to decreasing energy in the carbon core. As can be seen in Fig. 5(c) and (d), the PL intensities decline in both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs due to the addition of Cd2+ ions. However, based on Fig. 5(c), the quenching effect by carboxyl functionalized CQDs is more significant compared to amino functionalized CQDs (Fig. 5(d)) because carboxyl is more electronegative than amine group, forming carboxylate complex structure thus leading to enhancement of force of attraction to the cations (Cd2+) [50]. The interaction between CQDs and Cd2+ ions leads to electron transfer from the CQDs to metal ions. The electrostatic interaction between negatively charged CQDs and positively charged metal ions plays the important role in PL quenching process. The availability of adsorption site on cationic Cd2+ surface allows the adhesion of CQDs, thus leads to blocking of recombination of electron and hole in CQDs’ structure and consequently reduces the PL intensity [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time and amount of functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the removal efficiency of Cd2+ ions under certain contact times and volume of functionalized CQDs, respectively. The results suggest that both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs have notable potential in Cd2+ ions removal from aqueous solution. The increasing contact time allows more adhesion of Cd2+ ions onto the CQDs’ surface. Based on Fig. 6(a), both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs are able to remove above 70 % of cadmium even after only 20 min of mixing CQDs and cadmium solution. However, the cadmium removal does not show significant increment within the time interval towards the end of one hour due to the occupied active sites on the CQDs’ surface for both functionalized CQDs. Apart from that, the binding energy between CQDs and Cd2+ ions tends to decrease upon elongating the contact time due to possible aggregation of Cd2+ ions adsorbed on the CQDs’ surface [17]. This leads to the competition between metal-metal and metal-surface interactions, hence binding energy becomes weaker. The agglomeration of particles consisting of CQDs and cadmium enhances the formation of larger ligand as well as reducing the band gap between HOMO and LUMO in the CQDs’ structure [17].

In another case, both functionalized CQDs with volume variation are utilized for cadmium removal to prove CQDs potential in heavy metal ion removal as shown in Fig. 6(b). Both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs can remove more than 70 % of cadmium due to the strong interaction between the carbon nanoparticles and the metal ion along increasing the amount of CQDs. Higher amount of CQDs implies that more active sites are available for heavy metal ion adhesion, thus leads to its easy removal. It can be seen that amino functionalized CQDs can remove higher amount of cadmium compared to carboxyl functionalized CQDs. This is because the amount of functionalization agent for amino functionalized CQDs namely EDA is higher compared to ascorbic acid for carboxyl functionalized during CQDs’ synthesis process. More adsorptive sites are available for the chances of interaction between negatively charged CQDs and the metal cations. Therefore, binding energy for metal-CQDs interaction becomes stronger. The formation of metal cluster then will eventually diffuse across the CQDs’ surface and consequently leading to easy removal of the metal ions [17]. In addition, the reduction potential in cadmium solution enhances the ability of CQDs to remove Cd2+ ions from its aqueous solution [51]. The performance of CQDs in heavy metal removal as reported in a study by Aji et al. [52], stated that increment of contact time and amount of adsorbent enhance the electrostatic interaction with the metal cation. In addition, a study reported by Yang et al. [61] has proven that implementation of CQDs in cadmium removal shows promising performance in which about 85 % of cadmium removal could be achieved in first 20 min of contact time.

Pseudo-first and pseudo-second order kinetic models are analyzed to determine which model fits the adsorption process well. The linear form of pseudo-first and pseudo-second order models are expressed as in Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively:

From Fig. 6(d), cadmium removal by using both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs fit the pseudo-second order model well which also similar to a study by Ma et al. [53] that suggested cadmium removal involves one cadmium ion adsorbed onto two sites of the adsorbent. The linear correlation is fitted with R2 = 0.9998 and R2 = 0.9997, respectively. The result obtained also suits a study made by Nagaraj et al. [54] which reported that adsorption of chromium (IV) ion, (Cr6+) obeys pseudo-second order kinetic model with maximum adsorption capacity of 934.61 mg/g. The parameters obtained from both kinetic models in this study are presented in Table 2 :

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial concentration of cadmium solution
ce:section.ce:para: The percentage removal of cadmium tends to decrease when cadmium initial concentration increases as shown in Fig. 7 (a). When initial concentration of cadmium solution increases from 20 to 125 ppm, the percentage removal of cadmium decreases from 81.5 to 76.6% for amino functionalized CQDs while in carboxyl functionalized CQDs, the percentage removal reduces from 79.5 to 76.4 %. According to Fig. 7(a), at the initial stage in which 20 ppm of cadmium solution is used, the percentage removal when using amino functionalized CQDs is higher compared to the carboxyl functionalized CQDs due to higher amount of EDA during synthesis process. This helps in enhancing the availability of active sites on the CQDs’ surface for the adsorption to take place. The number of adsorption sites is assumed to be constant, indicating that the removal efficiency of metal ion decreases upon increasing the initial concentration of cadmium [55].

Numerous studies used various natural based adsorbents for cadmium removal and the comparison of adsorption capacities of the adsorbents are presented in Table 3 while adsorption capacity against initial cadmium concentration is shown in Fig. 7(b). Coal fly ashes were used in cadmium removal as presented in a study by Ma et al. [53], having adsorption capacity of 200 mg/g. Bamboo charcoal is one of the of the low cost adsorbents for removal of cadmium with adsorption capacity of around 12 mg/g [56]. A green synthesis of iron nanoparticles with tangerine peel extract was also used in cadmium removal with capacity of 10.9 mg/g [57]. In addition, olive stone activated carbon was able to remove cadmium with capacity of 11.72 mg/g [58]. From theses certain investigations, it is shown that removal of cadmium using rice-husked derived CQDs has a significant improvement in terms of the adsorption capacity. This condition may be due to higher dispersibility of CQDs in the solution as well as the additional functional groups during functionalization of CQDs with both carboxyl and amine groups.

Isotherms are significant in quantifying the amount of cadmium adsorbed onto the CQDs’ surface. Most common isotherms used are Langmuir and Freundlich [21]. Langmuir isotherm described the adsorbent that have homogeneous active sites to bond with monolayer molecules without any interaction between the adjacent molecules. On the other hand, Freundlich isotherm was based on the assumption that there are heterogeneous sites on the adsorbent and there is an interaction between adsorbed molecules. The adsorption also happens in multilayer instead of monolayer [21]. In Freundlich isotherm, the amount of cadmium adsorbed onto CQDs increases when the concentration of cadmium increases. However, the removal efficiency tends to decrease due to reaching of equilibrium and lesser adsorptive sites on the surface of adsorbent. The linear expressions of Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms are presented in Eqs. (5) and (6), respectively:

. Based on Fig. 7(c), the cadmium removal by using both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs fit the Freundlich isotherm well with R2 = 0.9899 and R2 = 0.9798, respectively, having a similar result obtained by Ciotta et al. [60] for lead (Pb2+) ions removal. On the other hand, the maximum adsorption capacity in this study shows higher value than in a study by Yang et al. [61] with maximum adsorption capacity of 303 mg/g for cadmium removal by SiO2-CDs nanoparticles.

To study the influence of pH towards the adsorption of metal ion, experiment was conducted using different solution pH values ranging from 2 to 10. Fig. 8 (a) shows that the higher the pH value, the higher the adsorption capacity for both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs to remove Cd2+ ions. The percentage removal of cadmium increases slowly from 75 to 76 % for carboxyl functionalized CQDs while amino functionalized CQDs enhances the removal from 75.8 to 76.8 % when using pH from 2 to 8. Beyond pH 8, cadmium exists as cadmium hydroxide and stable colloids start to form in the solution [53]. Hence, this enhances the ability of the CQDs to remove the metal ion [53]. This explains the condition of having higher removal of cadmium at pH 10.

However, carboxyl functionalized CQDs exhibits greater removal than amino due to higher electronegativity of carboxyl group than the amine group causing cadmium can be easily removed from the solution. Previous study by Ma et al. [53] reported that the rapid adsorption was due to higher adsorption sites available in the adsorbent. The effect of pH also can be related to surface charge of CQDs as CQDs gain net negative charges causing them to be electrostatically suitable for cadmium adsorption. In addition, cadmium removal favors weak acidic condition (pH around 6 and above) that assists in its ionization, hence the ions can be readily adsorbed onto the negatively charged surface of functionalized CQDs [62]. This explains the cadmium adsorption is enhanced in higher pH condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point Zero Charge (PZC) of functionalized CQDs and mechanism of cadmium removal
ce:section.ce:para: Point Zero Charge (PZC) of the adsorbent is determined in an experiment by implementing salt addition method, similar to a study made by M. Dharmendirakumar et al. [21]. PZC is the condition in which the final pH is equal to the initial pH. The result in Fig. 8(b) shows that the PZC values of both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs are likely the same due to their common surface structure. The lower and higher PZC values give information on the CQDs’ surface condition, correspond to the predominance of positively charged and negatively charged CQDs, respectively [16,63]. In this study, the result shows that 8.1 is the PZC value for both functionalized CQDs which validates the result in the effect of pH towards the adsorption of cadmium from its aqueous solution. Above the PZC value, the adsorption of cadmium is enhanced due to availability of more negative charges on the CQDs’ surface to allow metal cationic binding through electrostatic force of attraction [64]. A similar finding was also reported by Kim et al. [65], stated that in condition of above PZC value, the surface of adsorbent became highly negative due to deprotonation, causing the adsorption of strontium (Sr2+) ions to increase.

The mechanism involved in the cadmium removal by both functionalized CQDs is mainly caused by ion exchange and hydrogen bonding. The significant presence of functional groups such as carboxyl, -OH and amine groups contributes to the adsorption of Cd2+ ions to the CQDs. In the condition of having pH lower than PZC, hydrogen ions compete with cadmium ions to be adsorbed onto CQDs while in the condition of pH higher than the PZC value, cadmium may exist in both Cd2+ and Cd(OH)+ species, hence there are more chances for the cadmium species for exchange sites on CQDs [66]. Therefore, percentage removal of cadmium is higher when pH increases. In higher pH, the Cd2+ and Cd(OH)+ exchange hydrogen to build electrostatic force of attraction with carboxyl and amine groups in CQDs. In addition, hydrogen bonding may occur between the functional groups in CQDs with Cd(OH)+ species, forming more stable colloid in the solution. Hence, the removal of cadmium becomes easier in higher pH condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NaCl addition towards cadmium removal
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of NaCl addition towards the cadmium removal is also reported in this study. Fig. 8(c) shows that upon increasing concentration of sodium chloride (NaCl), the percentage removal of cadmium by using both carboxyl and amino CQDs decreases. This also implies that the adsorption capacity of functionalized CQDs decreases in higher salt concentration. The finding is similar to a study made by Adeli et al. [67] that using magnetite nanoparticles to remove copper, nickel and zinc from aqueous solution. The removal of cadmium decreases from 77 to 76.2 % for carboxyl and from 76.7 to 75% for amino functionalized CQDs when the concentration of salt increases from 0.01 M to 0.1 M. This is because salt concentration is proportionally correlated to the ionic strength of the solution [66]. The lowering performance of CQDs with increasing salt concentration may be a result from a competition between cations from the salt and cadmium ion to bind with adsorptive sites of the negatively charged CQDs. In general, when the CQDs are in contact with the metal ion, an electrostatic interaction will be generated to enhance the adsorption process [66]. Hence, the variation of electrolyte concentration which is by implementing salt addition with different concentration will affect the sorbent- sorbate interaction.

This study reports that variation of amount of functionalization agents during CQDs’ synthesis are able to tune the photoluminescence properties of CQDs for various applications. Both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs exhibit tunable photoluminescence intensity as well as increasing carbon and nitrogen elements when higher amount of ascorbic acid and EDA are used as functionalization agents during the synthesis. The XPS analysis signifies that CQDs can be well functionalized to observe their properties. Hence, this study proves that the photoluminescence properties of CQDs is tunable with regards to variation in amount of functionalization agent during the synthesis process. The prepared functionalized CQDs are also able to detect and remove cadmium more than 70 % from the aqueous solution based on certain parameters variation. The removal of cadmium by using both carboxyl and amino functionalized CQDs also obeys the pseudo-second order kinetic model with R2 = 0.9998. They also obey Freundlich isotherm with R2 = 0.9899 and R2 = 0.9798, respectively.
